title: The Rain on the Tin Roof
text_zh: 我租住的老房子有一个铁皮屋顶。下雨的时候，雨点打在铁皮上，发出清脆而密集的声响，像是有人用无数根小鼓槌在上面同时敲击。起初我觉得吵，后来渐渐觉得这是一种奢侈。住在高层公寓里的人听不见雨声，他们只能通过窗玻璃上的水痕判断外面在下雨。但我能听见每一滴雨落下来的重量和速度。暴雨来的时候声如擂鼓，密密麻麻的雨点像是在朗诵一首急促的诗。小雨则像轻声细语，滴滴答答，间歇性地停顿，仿佛在思考下一句该说什么。最好听的是雨快停的时候，雨点变得稀疏，每一滴之间的间隔越来越长，像是一段音乐慢慢减弱，最后只剩下屋檐滴水的声音，节奏缓慢而规律。有一个雨夜我躺在床上，闭着眼睛只听雨声，忽然觉得自己像是住在一个巨大的乐器里面。这座城市的夜晚有太多噪音——空调外机、汽车鸣笛、夜宵摊的喧哗。但铁皮屋顶上的雨声把这些全都盖住了，只留下最原始的声响。也许有一天这栋老房子会被拆掉，但我会永远记得这种声音。
text_pinyin: Wǒ zūzhù de lǎo fángzi yǒu yí gè tiěpí wūdǐng. Xià yǔ de shíhou, yǔdiǎn dǎ zài tiěpí shàng, fāchū qīngcuì ér mìjí de shēngxiǎng, xiàng shì yǒu rén yòng wúshù gēn xiǎo gǔchuí zài shàngmiàn tóngshí qiāojī. Qǐchū wǒ juéde chǎo, hòulái jiànjiàn juéde zhè shì yì zhǒng shēchǐ. Zhù zài gāocéng gōngyù lǐ de rén tīng bú jiàn yǔshēng, tāmen zhǐ néng tōngguò chuāng bōli shàng de shuǐhén pànduàn wàimiàn zài xià yǔ. Dàn wǒ néng tīng jiàn měi yì dī yǔ luò xiàlái de zhòngliàng hé sùdù. Bàoyǔ lái de shíhou shēng rú léigǔ, mìmìmámá de yǔdiǎn xiàng shì zài lǎngsòng yì shǒu jícù de shī. Xiǎo yǔ zé xiàng qīngshēng xìyǔ, dīdī-dādā, jiànxiē xìng de tíngdùn, fǎngfú zài sīkǎo xià yí jù gāi shuō shénme. Zuì hǎotīng de shì yǔ kuài tíng de shíhou, yǔdiǎn biàn de xīshū, měi yì dī zhījiān de jiàngé yuè lái yuè cháng, xiàng shì yí duàn yīnyuè mànmàn jiǎnruò, zuìhòu zhǐ shèng xià wūyán dī shuǐ de shēngyīn, jiézòu huǎnmàn ér guīlǜ. Yǒu yí gè yǔ yè wǒ tǎng zài chuáng shàng, bì zhe yǎnjing zhǐ tīng yǔshēng, hūrán juéde zìjǐ xiàng shì zhù zài yí gè jùdà de yuèqì lǐmiàn. Zhè zuò chéngshì de yèwǎn yǒu tài duō zàoyīn — kōngtiáo wàijī, qìchē míngdí, yèxiāo tān de xuānhuá. Dàn tiěpí wūdǐng shàng de yǔshēng bǎ zhèxiē quán dōu gài zhù le, zhǐ liú xià zuì yuánshǐ de shēngxiǎng. Yěxǔ yǒu yì tiān zhè dòng lǎo fángzi huì bèi chāi diào, dàn wǒ huì yǒngyuǎn jìde zhè zhǒng shēngyīn.
text_en: The old house I rent has a tin roof. When it rains, the drops strike the tin with crisp, dense sounds, as if someone is drumming with countless small mallets simultaneously. At first I found it noisy, but gradually I came to see it as a luxury. People in high-rise apartments can't hear the rain; they can only tell it's raining from the water trails on their window glass. But I can hear the weight and speed of every raindrop falling. When a downpour arrives, it thunders like war drums, the dense raindrops reciting a rapid poem. Light rain is like whispering, pit-a-pat, pausing intermittently as if thinking about what to say next. The most beautiful moment is when the rain is about to stop — the drops become sparse, the intervals between each one growing longer, like music gradually fading, until only the sound of water dripping from the eaves remains, slow and rhythmic. One rainy night I lay in bed with my eyes closed just listening to the rain, and suddenly felt as if I were living inside an enormous musical instrument. This city's nights have too much noise — air conditioning units, car horns, the clamor of late-night food stalls. But the rain on the tin roof drowns it all out, leaving only the most primal sounds. Perhaps one day this old house will be demolished, but I will always remember this sound.

title: The Shoe Repairman's Hands
text_zh: 地铁站出口的修鞋匠有一双让人过目不忘的手。那双手粗大、黝黑，指节突出，手背上的青筋像是老树的根。但那双手动起来的时候，却有一种出人意料的精细。他用锥子在鞋底穿孔的动作，力道恰到好处——既要穿透坚韧的橡胶底，又不能刺穿鞋垫。他穿针引线的速度极快，粗糙的手指灵活得不像是属于一个六十多岁的人。我坐在他对面的小板凳上等着修鞋，忍不住一直看他的手。他感觉到了我的目光，笑了笑说：「好看吧？修了四十年鞋，就剩这双手还值得看。」他给我看他左手食指上的一道深深的疤痕，说是年轻的时候不小心被刀片割的，「那时候手还嫩，现在刀片都割不进去了。」他把修好的鞋递给我，我低头看，缝合的针脚均匀整齐，比原来的机器缝线还要漂亮。我付了十五块钱，他找零的时候，我注意到他从一个铁盒子里小心地挑出硬币，动作轻柔得像是在触碰什么易碎的东西。一双能修补粗糙事物的手，在处理细小事物的时候，反而最温柔。
text_pinyin: Dìtiě zhàn chūkǒu de xiū xié jiàng yǒu yì shuāng ràng rén guòmù-bùwàng de shǒu. Nà shuāng shǒu cūdà, yǒuhēi, zhǐjié tūchū, shǒubèi shàng de qīngjīn xiàng shì lǎo shù de gēn. Dàn nà shuāng shǒu dòng qǐlái de shíhou, què yǒu yì zhǒng chūrén-yìliào de jīngxì. Tā yòng zhuīzi zài xiédǐ chuān kǒng de dòngzuò, lìdào qiàdào-hǎochù — jì yào chuāntòu jiānrèn de xiàngjiāo dǐ, yòu bù néng cì chuān xiédiàn. Tā chuānzhēn-yǐnxiàn de sùdù jí kuài, cūcāo de shǒuzhǐ línghuó de bú xiàng shì shǔyú yí gè liùshí duō suì de rén. Wǒ zuò zài tā duìmiàn de xiǎo bǎndèng shàng děng zhe xiū xié, rěn bú zhù yìzhí kàn tā de shǒu. Tā gǎnjué dào le wǒ de mùguāng, xiào le xiào shuō: 「Hǎokàn ba? Xiū le sìshí nián xié, jiù shèng zhè shuāng shǒu hái zhíde kàn.」 Tā gěi wǒ kàn tā zuǒ shǒu shízhǐ shàng de yí dào shēnshēn de bāhén, shuō shì niánqīng de shíhou bù xiǎoxīn bèi dāopiàn gē de, 「nà shíhou shǒu hái nèn, xiànzài dāopiàn dōu gē bú jìnqù le.」 Tā bǎ xiū hǎo de xié dì gěi wǒ, wǒ dītóu kàn, fénghé de zhēnjiǎo jūnyún zhěngqí, bǐ yuánlái de jīqì féngxiàn hái yào piàoliang. Wǒ fù le shíwǔ kuài qián, tā zhǎo líng de shíhou, wǒ zhùyì dào tā cóng yí gè tiě hézi lǐ xiǎoxīn de tiāo chū yìngbì, dòngzuò qīngróu de xiàng shì zài chùpèng shénme yìsuì de dōngxi. Yì shuāng néng xiūbǔ cūcāo shìwù de shǒu, zài chǔlǐ xìxiǎo shìwù de shíhou, fǎn'ér zuì wēnróu.
text_en: The shoe repairman at the subway exit has a pair of unforgettable hands. They are large, dark, with prominent knuckles, veins on the backs like old tree roots. But when those hands move, they possess an unexpected delicacy. The way he pierces holes in a sole with an awl is perfectly calibrated — enough force to penetrate tough rubber, but not enough to puncture the insole. He threads the needle with remarkable speed, his rough fingers nimble beyond what you'd expect from a man in his sixties. I sat on the small stool across from him waiting for my shoes, unable to stop watching his hands. He sensed my gaze and smiled: 'Something to look at, right? Forty years of shoe repair, and these hands are all that's left worth seeing.' He showed me a deep scar on his left index finger, from a blade slip when he was young. 'My hands were still soft then. Now even a blade can't cut in.' He handed me the repaired shoes. I looked down — the stitching was even and neat, more beautiful than the original machine seams. I paid fifteen yuan. When he made change, I noticed him carefully picking coins from a tin box, his movements as gentle as if he were touching something fragile. A pair of hands capable of mending rough things becomes gentlest when handling small ones.

title: The Fishpond in the Park
text_zh: 公园东边有一个不大的鱼塘，周围种着几棵垂柳。每天下午，总有五六个老人坐在柳树下钓鱼。他们自带折叠椅、保温杯和收音机，一坐就是好几个小时。鱼塘里的鱼并不多，据说放进去的都是最便宜的鲫鱼，偶尔才能钓上一条。但这似乎完全不影响他们的热情。我观察了很久，发现他们钓鱼的方式各不相同。穿格子衬衫的老王习惯用两根鱼竿，左右开弓，像是在下一盘棋。戴草帽的老张只用一根鱼竿，但每隔十分钟就换一种鱼饵，极其讲究。最有意思的是坐在最东边的老李，他根本不挂鱼饵，空钩入水，却比谁都认真地盯着浮标。有人问他这样怎么钓得到鱼，他说：「我又不是来钓鱼的。」大家都笑了。后来我才明白，鱼塘对他们来说不是一个钓鱼的地方，而是一个聚会的借口。他们需要的不是鱼，而是一个理由，让自己可以安安静静地坐在户外，和老朋友待在一起，什么话都不说也不觉得尴尬。鱼竿只是一种姿态，真正在钓的，是一种正在消逝的闲适。
text_pinyin: Gōngyuán dōngbiān yǒu yí gè bú dà de yútáng, zhōuwéi zhòng zhe jǐ kē chuíliǔ. Měitiān xiàwǔ, zǒng yǒu wǔ liù gè lǎorén zuò zài liǔshù xià diào yú. Tāmen zì dài zhédié yǐ, bǎowēn bēi hé shōuyīnjī, yí zuò jiù shì hǎo jǐ gè xiǎoshí. Yútáng lǐ de yú bìng bù duō, jùshuō fàng jìnqù de dōu shì zuì piányi de jìyú, ǒu'ěr cái néng diào shàng yì tiáo. Dàn zhè sìhū wánquán bù yǐngxiǎng tāmen de rèqíng. Wǒ guānchá le hěn jiǔ, fāxiàn tāmen diào yú de fāngshì gè bù xiāngtóng. Chuān gézi chènshān de Lǎo Wáng xíguàn yòng liǎng gēn yúgān, zuǒyòu-kāigōng, xiàng shì zài xià yì pán qí. Dài cǎomào de Lǎo Zhāng zhǐ yòng yì gēn yúgān, dàn měi gé shí fēnzhōng jiù huàn yì zhǒng yú'ěr, jíqí jiǎngjiu. Zuì yǒu yìsi de shì zuò zài zuì dōngbiān de Lǎo Lǐ, tā gēnběn bú guà yú'ěr, kōng gōu rù shuǐ, què bǐ shéi dōu rènzhēn de dīng zhe fúbiāo. Yǒu rén wèn tā zhèyàng zěnme diào de dào yú, tā shuō: 「Wǒ yòu bú shì lái diào yú de.」 Dàjiā dōu xiào le. Hòulái wǒ cái míngbai, yútáng duì tāmen lái shuō bú shì yí gè diào yú de dìfang, ér shì yí gè jùhuì de jièkǒu. Tāmen xūyào de bú shì yú, ér shì yí gè lǐyóu, ràng zìjǐ kěyǐ ān'ānjìngjìng de zuò zài hùwài, hé lǎo péngyou dāi zài yìqǐ, shénme huà dōu bù shuō yě bù juéde gāngà. Yúgān zhǐ shì yì zhǒng zītài, zhēnzhèng zài diào de, shì yì zhǒng zhèngzài xiāoshì de xiánshì.
text_en: On the east side of the park is a small fishpond, surrounded by a few weeping willows. Every afternoon, five or six old men sit under the willows and fish. They bring their own folding chairs, thermos cups, and radios, sitting for hours at a stretch. There aren't many fish in the pond — reportedly just the cheapest crucian carp, and only occasionally does someone catch one. But this doesn't seem to dampen their enthusiasm at all. After watching for a long time, I noticed they each fish differently. Lao Wang in his plaid shirt uses two rods at once, working both sides like he's playing a game of chess. Lao Zhang in his straw hat uses just one rod but changes bait every ten minutes, extremely particular. The most interesting is Lao Li, sitting at the far east — he doesn't even use bait, casting an empty hook into the water, yet watches the bobber more intently than anyone. When someone asked how he could catch fish that way, he said, 'I'm not here to catch fish.' Everyone laughed. Later I understood: for them, the fishpond isn't a place for fishing but an excuse to gather. What they need isn't fish, but a reason to sit quietly outdoors with old friends, where saying nothing at all doesn't feel awkward. The fishing rod is just a posture; what they're really fishing for is a kind of leisure that is vanishing.

title: The Sound of Sweeping
text_zh: 每天早上五点半，我都会被一种声音叫醒——沙沙，沙沙——是楼下环卫工人扫地的声音。竹扫帚划过柏油路面，发出一种干燥的、有节奏的摩擦声。冬天的时候这声音格外清晰，因为清晨的空气冷而通透，声音传得很远。我曾经为此烦躁过，甚至想过打投诉电话。但有一天我决定早起，下楼去看看这个每天打扰我睡眠的人。她是一个五十岁左右的女人，穿着橘黄色的反光背心，戴着口罩，只露出一双眼睛。她扫地的动作很有韵律——先是从路边向中间聚拢，然后沿着一条无形的线把落叶和灰尘推到一处。整个过程像是一种舞蹈的重复练习。她看见我站在那里，停下来点了点头，然后继续扫。那天之后我再也没有觉得那个声音是噪音。它变成了一种提示——提示我这座城市在我还在睡觉的时候，有人已经开始为它清理。每一个整洁的街道背后，都有一个天不亮就出门的人。而我能为他们做的，不过是在经过的时候多看一眼，记住他们的存在。
text_pinyin: Měitiān zǎoshang wǔ diǎn bàn, wǒ dōu huì bèi yì zhǒng shēngyīn jiào xǐng — shāshā, shāshā — shì lóu xià huánwèi gōngrén sǎo dì de shēngyīn. Zhú sàozhou huá guò bǎiyóu lùmiàn, fāchū yì zhǒng gānzào de, yǒu jiézòu de mócā shēng. Dōngtiān de shíhou zhè shēngyīn géwài qīngxī, yīnwèi qīngchén de kōngqì lěng ér tōngtòu, shēngyīn chuán de hěn yuǎn. Wǒ céngjīng wèi cǐ fánzào guò, shènzhì xiǎng guò dǎ tóusù diànhuà. Dàn yǒu yì tiān wǒ juédìng zǎo qǐ, xià lóu qù kàn kàn zhège měitiān dǎrǎo wǒ shuìmián de rén. Tā shì yí gè wǔshí suì zuǒyòu de nǚrén, chuān zhe júhuángsè de fǎnguāng bèixīn, dài zhe kǒuzhào, zhǐ lùchū yì shuāng yǎnjing. Tā sǎo dì de dòngzuò hěn yǒu yùnlǜ — xiān shì cóng lù biān xiàng zhōngjiān jùlǒng, ránhòu yán zhe yì tiáo wúxíng de xiàn bǎ luòyè hé huīchén tuī dào yí chù. Zhěnggè guòchéng xiàng shì yì zhǒng wǔdǎo de chóngfù liànxí. Tā kàn jiàn wǒ zhàn zài nàlǐ, tíng xiàlái diǎn le diǎn tóu, ránhòu jìxù sǎo. Nà tiān zhīhòu wǒ zài yě méiyǒu juéde nàge shēngyīn shì zàoyīn. Tā biàn chéng le yì zhǒng tíshì — tíshì wǒ zhè zuò chéngshì zài wǒ hái zài shuìjiào de shíhou, yǒu rén yǐjīng kāishǐ wèi tā qīnglǐ. Měi yí gè zhěngjié de jiēdào bèihòu, dōu yǒu yí gè tiān bú liàng jiù chūmén de rén. Ér wǒ néng wèi tāmen zuò de, búguò shì zài jīngguò de shíhou duō kàn yì yǎn, jìzhù tāmen de cúnzài.
text_en: Every morning at five-thirty, I'm woken by a sound — swish, swish — the street sweeper downstairs. A bamboo broom dragging across asphalt, making a dry, rhythmic scraping. In winter the sound is especially clear, because the cold morning air is crisp and transparent, carrying the sound far. I used to be irritated by it, even considered calling in a complaint. But one day I decided to get up early and go downstairs to see this person who disturbed my sleep every day. She was a woman of about fifty, wearing an orange reflective vest and a face mask, only her eyes showing. Her sweeping had a real rhythm — first gathering from the roadside toward the center, then pushing leaves and dust along an invisible line to a single point. The whole process was like a repeated dance rehearsal. She saw me standing there, stopped and nodded, then continued sweeping. After that day I never thought of the sound as noise again. It became a reminder — that while I was still sleeping, someone was already cleaning this city. Behind every tidy street is a person who leaves home before dawn. And all I can do for them is glance once more as I pass by, and remember that they exist.

title: The Elderly Couple on the Bench
text_zh: 每天上午十点，公园入口旁边的长椅上总会出现一对老两口。他们来的时候，老先生走在前面，手里拎着一个布袋，老太太走在后面，步伐稍慢。到了长椅前，老先生会先用手帕把椅面擦一遍，然后扶着老太太坐下。他从布袋里拿出两个保温杯、一包花生和一份报纸。老太太喝茶，老先生看报。他们很少说话，偶尔老先生会念一段新闻给老太太听，老太太就嗯一声，表示知道了。有时候老太太会轻轻拍一下老先生的手背，指一指远处某棵开了花的树。老先生就抬头看看，点点头。整个过程安静得像是一幅画的两个局部在缓慢移动。十一点半的时候，老先生会把东西收进布袋，站起来拍拍裤子，然后伸出手。老太太握住他的手站起来，两个人按照来的时候相反的顺序走回去——老太太在前，老先生在后，走得更慢一些。我看了他们整整一个春天。一对七八十岁的老人，用一种几乎无声的方式彼此照顾，这种安静不是冷淡，而是经过了所有言语之后剩下的默契。最深的温柔，往往看起来最平淡。
text_pinyin: Měitiān shàngwǔ shí diǎn, gōngyuán rùkǒu pángbiān de chángyǐ shàng zǒng huì chūxiàn yí duì lǎo liǎng kǒu. Tāmen lái de shíhou, lǎo xiānsheng zǒu zài qiánmiàn, shǒu lǐ līn zhe yí gè bù dài, lǎo tàitai zǒu zài hòumiàn, bùfá shāo màn. Dào le chángyǐ qián, lǎo xiānsheng huì xiān yòng shǒupà bǎ yǐ miàn cā yí biàn, ránhòu fú zhe lǎo tàitai zuò xià. Tā cóng bù dài lǐ ná chū liǎng gè bǎowēn bēi, yì bāo huāshēng hé yí fèn bàozhǐ. Lǎo tàitai hē chá, lǎo xiānsheng kàn bào. Tāmen hěn shǎo shuōhuà, ǒu'ěr lǎo xiānsheng huì niàn yí duàn xīnwén gěi lǎo tàitai tīng, lǎo tàitai jiù ēn yì shēng, biǎoshì zhīdào le. Yǒu shíhou lǎo tàitai huì qīngqīng pāi yíxià lǎo xiānsheng de shǒubèi, zhǐ yi zhǐ yuǎnchù mǒu kē kāi le huā de shù. Lǎo xiānsheng jiù táitóu kàn kàn, diǎndiǎn tóu. Zhěnggè guòchéng ānjìng de xiàng shì yí fú huà de liǎng gè júbù zài huǎnmàn yídòng. Shíyī diǎn bàn de shíhou, lǎo xiānsheng huì bǎ dōngxi shōu jìn bù dài, zhàn qǐlái pāipai kùzi, ránhòu shēn chū shǒu. Lǎo tàitai wò zhù tā de shǒu zhàn qǐlái, liǎng gè rén ànzhào lái de shíhou xiāngfǎn de shùnxù zǒu huíqu — lǎo tàitai zài qián, lǎo xiānsheng zài hòu, zǒu de gèng màn yìxiē. Wǒ kàn le tāmen zhěngzhěng yí gè chūntiān. Yí duì qībāshí suì de lǎorén, yòng yì zhǒng jīhū wúshēng de fāngshì bǐcǐ zhàogù, zhè zhǒng ānjìng bú shì lěngdàn, ér shì jīngguò le suǒyǒu yányǔ zhīhòu shèng xià de mòqì. Zuì shēn de wēnróu, wǎngwǎng kàn qǐlái zuì píngdàn.
text_en: Every morning at ten, an elderly couple appears on the bench by the park entrance. When they arrive, the old gentleman walks ahead carrying a cloth bag, the old woman follows behind at a slightly slower pace. At the bench, the gentleman first wipes the seat with a handkerchief, then helps his wife sit down. He takes out two thermos cups, a bag of peanuts, and a newspaper from the cloth bag. She drinks tea; he reads the paper. They rarely speak. Occasionally the gentleman reads a piece of news to her, and she hums an acknowledgment. Sometimes she gently taps the back of his hand and points to some distant tree in bloom. He looks up, nods. The whole scene is as quiet as two parts of a painting slowly shifting. At eleven-thirty, the gentleman packs everything back into the bag, stands up, brushes off his trousers, and extends his hand. She takes it and stands. The two walk back in reverse order from when they came — she in front, he behind, a bit more slowly. I watched them for an entire spring. A couple in their seventies or eighties caring for each other in a nearly wordless way. This quiet isn't coldness, but the understanding that remains after all words have been spoken. The deepest tenderness often looks the most ordinary.

title: The Convenience Store at Three AM
text_zh: 凌晨三点的便利店有一种白天没有的气质。日光灯把店内照得雪亮，和外面漆黑的街道形成了鲜明的对比，像是黑暗中的一个发光的盒子。我因为失眠走了进去，货架之间只有一个店员在整理商品。她看见我进来，面无表情地说了一句「欢迎光临」，声音带着一种机械的疲惫。我在饮料柜前站了很久，拿起又放下，最后选了一罐温热的咖啡牛奶。收银台旁边坐着一个穿外卖服的小伙子，面前摆着一碗关东煮，他吃得很慢，好像在珍惜这段难得的休息时间。角落里还有一个穿西装的中年男人，领带松了，靠着杂志架在翻一本摄影画册，翻得很慢，像是不急着去任何地方。凌晨三点的便利店就像一个临时的避难所。来这里的人都不是因为需要买什么东西，而是需要一个灯火通明的、有人存在的空间。在这个所有正常场所都关闭的时间点，便利店的灯光提供了一种最低限度的陪伴——不打扰你，但让你知道你不是一个人。我喝完咖啡，把空罐扔进垃圾桶，推门走入夜色。身后的自动门缓缓合上，那片光留在了原处。
text_pinyin: Língchén sān diǎn de biànlì diàn yǒu yì zhǒng báitiān méiyǒu de qìzhì. Rìguāngdēng bǎ diàn nèi zhào de xuě liàng, hé wàimiàn qīhēi de jiēdào xíngchéng le xiānmíng de duìbǐ, xiàng shì hēi'àn zhōng de yí gè fāguāng de hézi. Wǒ yīnwèi shīmián zǒu le jìnqù, huòjià zhījiān zhǐ yǒu yí gè diànyuán zài zhěnglǐ shāngpǐn. Tā kàn jiàn wǒ jìnlái, miànwú-biǎoqíng de shuō le yí jù 「huānyíng guānglín」, shēngyīn dài zhe yì zhǒng jīxiè de píbèi. Wǒ zài yǐnliào guì qián zhàn le hěn jiǔ, ná qǐ yòu fàng xià, zuìhòu xuǎn le yì guàn wēnrè de kāfēi niúnǎi. Shōuyín tái pángbiān zuò zhe yí gè chuān wàimài fú de xiǎohuǒzi, miànqián bǎi zhe yì wǎn guāndōngzhǔ, tā chī de hěn màn, hǎoxiàng zài zhēnxī zhè duàn nándé de xiūxi shíjiān. Jiǎoluò lǐ háiyǒu yí gè chuān xīzhuāng de zhōngnián nánrén, lǐngdài sōng le, kào zhe zázhì jià zài fān yì běn shèyǐng huàcè, fān de hěn màn, xiàng shì bú jí zhe qù rènhé dìfang. Língchén sān diǎn de biànlì diàn jiù xiàng yí gè línshí de bìnànsuǒ. Lái zhèlǐ de rén dōu bú shì yīnwèi xūyào mǎi shénme dōngxi, ér shì xūyào yí gè dēnghuǒ-tōngmíng de, yǒu rén cúnzài de kōngjiān. Zài zhège suǒyǒu zhèngcháng chǎngsuǒ dōu guānbì de shíjiān diǎn, biànlì diàn de dēngguāng tígōng le yì zhǒng zuì dī xiàndù de péibàn — bù dǎrǎo nǐ, dàn ràng nǐ zhīdào nǐ bú shì yí gè rén. Wǒ hē wán kāfēi, bǎ kōng guàn rēng jìn lājī tǒng, tuī mén zǒu rù yèsè. Shēnhòu de zìdòng mén huǎnhuǎn hé shàng, nà piàn guāng liú zài le yuán chù.
text_en: A convenience store at three in the morning has a quality that daytime lacks. Fluorescent lights blaze the interior white, forming a sharp contrast with the pitch-dark street outside, like a glowing box in the darkness. Unable to sleep, I wandered in. Between the shelves, a single clerk was restocking goods. Seeing me enter, she said 'welcome' expressionlessly, her voice carrying a mechanical fatigue. I stood before the drink cooler a long time, picking up and putting down, finally choosing a warm can of cafe au lait. Beside the register sat a young man in a delivery uniform, a bowl of oden in front of him, eating slowly as if savoring this rare moment of rest. In the corner, a middle-aged man in a suit, tie loosened, leaned against the magazine rack flipping through a photography book, turning pages slowly, as if in no hurry to go anywhere. A convenience store at three AM is like a temporary shelter. Nobody comes here because they need to buy something — they need a brightly lit space where other people exist. At this hour when all normal establishments are closed, the convenience store's light offers a minimal kind of company — it doesn't intrude, but lets you know you're not alone. I finished my coffee, tossed the empty can in the trash, and pushed through the door into the night. Behind me the automatic door slowly closed, that patch of light remaining where it was.

title: The Neighbor's Piano
text_zh: 搬到这个小区之后，每天晚上七点到八点之间，我都能听到隔壁传来的钢琴声。弹的人水平不高，经常在同一个地方卡住，反反复复地练同一个小节。一开始我判断是个孩子在练琴，因为曲目都是初级的练习曲，而且速度忽快忽慢，明显缺乏控制力。但有一天晚上，钢琴声突然变了。不再是练习曲，而是一首我熟悉的老歌——邓丽君的《月亮代表我的心》。弹得并不流畅，但每一个音符都带着一种小心翼翼的深情，像是在用手指讲一个故事。之后的每个周末晚上，那个人都会弹一首老歌。有时是《恰似你的温柔》，有时是《甜蜜蜜》。平日里练基础，周末弹心事。后来有一次在电梯里，我遇到了隔壁的住户——一个头发花白的老先生。我忍不住问他是不是他在弹琴。他有些不好意思地说，退休之后开始学的，学了两年了。「年轻的时候没条件学，」他说，「现在学也不算晚吧。」他说他老伴生前最喜欢邓丽君的歌。我没有再问下去。回到家里，七点整，钢琴声准时响起，依旧磕磕绊绊，依旧认真。那些不完美的音符穿过墙壁传来，比任何完美的演奏都更动人。
text_pinyin: Bān dào zhège xiǎoqū zhīhòu, měitiān wǎnshang qī diǎn dào bā diǎn zhījiān, wǒ dōu néng tīng dào gébì chuán lái de gāngqín shēng. Tán de rén shuǐpíng bù gāo, jīngcháng zài tóng yí gè dìfang kǎ zhù, fǎnfǎn-fùfù de liàn tóng yí gè xiǎojié. Yì kāishǐ wǒ pànduàn shì gè háizi zài liàn qín, yīnwèi qǔmù dōu shì chūjí de liànxíqǔ, érqiě sùdù hū kuài hū màn, míngxiǎn quēfá kòngzhìlì. Dàn yǒu yì tiān wǎnshang, gāngqín shēng tūrán biàn le. Bú zài shì liànxíqǔ, ér shì yì shǒu wǒ shúxī de lǎo gē — Dèng Lìjūn de 《Yuèliang Dàibiǎo Wǒ de Xīn》. Tán de bìng bù liúchàng, dàn měi yí gè yīnfú dōu dài zhe yì zhǒng xiǎoxīn-yìyì de shēnqíng, xiàng shì zài yòng shǒuzhǐ jiǎng yí gè gùshi. Zhīhòu de měi gè zhōumò wǎnshang, nàge rén dōu huì tán yì shǒu lǎo gē. Yǒu shí shì 《Qiàsì Nǐ de Wēnróu》, yǒu shí shì 《Tiánmìmì》. Píngrì lǐ liàn jīchǔ, zhōumò tán xīnshì. Hòulái yǒu yí cì zài diàntī lǐ, wǒ yù dào le gébì de zhùhù — yí gè tóufa huābái de lǎo xiānsheng. Wǒ rěn bú zhù wèn tā shì bu shì tā zài tán qín. Tā yǒuxiē bù hǎoyìsi de shuō, tuìxiū zhīhòu kāishǐ xué de, xué le liǎng nián le. 「Niánqīng de shíhou méi tiáojiàn xué,」 tā shuō, 「xiànzài xué yě bú suàn wǎn ba.」 Tā shuō tā lǎobàn shēng qián zuì xǐhuan Dèng Lìjūn de gē. Wǒ méiyǒu zài wèn xiàqù. Huí dào jiā lǐ, qī diǎn zhěng, gāngqín shēng zhǔnshí xiǎng qǐ, yījiù kēkē-bànbàn, yījiù rènzhēn. Nàxiē bù wánměi de yīnfú chuān guò qiángbì chuán lái, bǐ rènhé wánměi de yǎnzòu dōu gèng dòngrén.
text_en: After moving to this neighborhood, every evening between seven and eight I can hear piano playing from next door. The player isn't very skilled, often getting stuck in the same spot, practicing the same passage over and over. At first I assumed it was a child practicing, since the pieces were beginner exercises and the tempo fluctuated, obviously lacking control. But one evening, the piano suddenly changed. No longer exercises, but an old song I recognized — Teresa Teng's 'The Moon Represents My Heart.' The playing wasn't smooth, but every note carried a careful, deep affection, as if telling a story with fingers. After that, every weekend evening, the person would play an old song. Sometimes 'Just Like Your Tenderness,' sometimes 'Tian Mi Mi.' Weekdays for fundamentals, weekends for what's in the heart. Later, I ran into the neighbor in the elevator — a grey-haired old gentleman. I couldn't help asking if he was the one playing. He said a little sheepishly that he'd started learning after retirement, two years ago. 'When I was young I didn't have the means to learn,' he said. 'It's not too late to start now, is it?' He said his late wife loved Teresa Teng's songs the most. I didn't ask further. Back in my apartment, at seven on the dot, the piano started up, still halting, still earnest. Those imperfect notes passing through the wall were more moving than any perfect performance.

title: The Underground Bookstore
text_zh: 在一栋写字楼的地下一层，藏着一家只有熟客才知道的书店。没有招牌，入口是一扇贴着「仓库」标志的铁门。推门进去，扑面而来的是旧书特有的纸香——一种混合了灰尘和时间的气味。书店的面积不大，大概六十平方米，但书架从地面一直延伸到天花板，中间的过道窄得只能侧身通过。老板是一个戴圆框眼镜的中年男人，总是坐在进门右手边的一张旧书桌后面，面前堆着几摞正在分类的书。他的分类方法很独特，不按出版社也不按作者，而是按照他自己发明的主题：「适合雨天读的书」「失恋时读的书」「辞职之前读的书」。他说这些分类来自二十年的观察，「人们找书的时候，其实不是在找一本具体的书，而是在找一种状态。」定价也很随意，他会翻开每本书看看有没有折痕和批注，如果上一个读者留下了认真的笔记，他反而会加价，因为「一本被认真读过的书，价值更高」。这家店不卖新书，不做网店，不接受移动支付。在这个一切都在追求效率的时代，它固执地保持着一种缓慢的、面对面的交易方式，像是对整个数字时代的温柔抵抗。
text_pinyin: Zài yí dòng xiězìlóu de dìxià yī céng, cáng zhe yì jiā zhǐ yǒu shúkè cái zhīdào de shūdiàn. Méiyǒu zhāopái, rùkǒu shì yí shàn tiē zhe 「cāngkù」 biāozhì de tiě mén. Tuī mén jìnqù, pūmiàn ér lái de shì jiù shū tèyǒu de zhǐxiāng — yì zhǒng hùnhé le huīchén hé shíjiān de qìwèi. Shūdiàn de miànjī bú dà, dàgài liùshí píngfāng mǐ, dàn shūjià cóng dìmiàn yìzhí yánshēn dào tiānhuābǎn, zhōngjiān de guòdào zhǎi de zhǐ néng cèshēn tōngguò. Lǎobǎn shì yí gè dài yuánkuàng yǎnjìng de zhōngnián nánrén, zǒng shì zuò zài jìn mén yòushǒu biān de yì zhāng jiù shūzhuō hòumiàn, miànqián duī zhe jǐ luò zhèngzài fēnlèi de shū. Tā de fēnlèi fāngfǎ hěn dútè, bú àn chūbǎnshè yě bú àn zuòzhě, ér shì ànzhào tā zìjǐ fāmíng de zhǔtí: 「shìhé yǔtiān dú de shū」「shīliàn shí dú de shū」「cízhí zhīqián dú de shū」. Tā shuō zhèxiē fēnlèi lái zì èrshí nián de guānchá, 「rénmen zhǎo shū de shíhou, qíshí bú shì zài zhǎo yì běn jùtǐ de shū, ér shì zài zhǎo yì zhǒng zhuàngtài.」 Dìngjià yě hěn suíyì, tā huì fānkāi měi běn shū kàn kàn yǒu méi yǒu zhéhén hé pīzhù, rúguǒ shàng yí gè dúzhě liú xià le rènzhēn de bǐjì, tā fǎn'ér huì jiājià, yīnwèi 「yì běn bèi rènzhēn dú guò de shū, jiàzhí gèng gāo」. Zhè jiā diàn bú mài xīn shū, bú zuò wǎngdiàn, bù jiēshòu yídòng zhīfù. Zài zhège yíqiè dōu zài zhuīqiú xiàolǜ de shídài, tā gùzhí de bǎochí zhe yì zhǒng huǎnmàn de, miànduìmiàn de jiāoyì fāngshì, xiàng shì duì zhěnggè shùzì shídài de wēnróu dǐkàng.
text_en: In the basement of an office building hides a bookstore known only to regulars. There is no sign; the entrance is an iron door marked 'Storage.' Push it open and you're met with the scent peculiar to old books — a smell mingling dust and time. The store is small, about sixty square meters, but bookshelves stretch from floor to ceiling, the aisles between them so narrow you must turn sideways to pass. The owner, a middle-aged man in round glasses, always sits behind an old desk to the right of the entrance, stacks of books being sorted piled before him. His categorization is unique — not by publisher or author, but by themes he invented himself: 'books to read on rainy days,' 'books to read after a breakup,' 'books to read before quitting your job.' He says these categories come from twenty years of observation: 'When people look for books, they're not really looking for a specific book — they're looking for a state of being.' His pricing is equally casual. He opens each book to check for dog-ears and annotations; if the previous reader left careful notes, he actually raises the price, because 'a book that has been read carefully is worth more.' This shop doesn't sell new books, doesn't have an online store, doesn't accept mobile payment. In an era chasing efficiency in everything, it stubbornly maintains a slow, face-to-face way of doing business, like a gentle resistance against the entire digital age.

title: The Hospital Cafeteria
text_zh: 医院的食堂和别的食堂不一样。这里的人吃饭时几乎不说话，即便说话也压低了声音，好像声音大一点就会惊扰到什么。穿白大褂的医生三五成群地坐在一起，用最快的速度把饭扒进嘴里，然后擦擦嘴就走了。他们的手术服上偶尔还能看到没来得及换下的手套痕迹。病人家属通常坐在靠窗的位置，面前的饭菜动了几口就不吃了，眼睛一直盯着手机，等着某个电话或者消息。有些人的眼圈是红的，但没有人哭出声来。食堂的菜品谈不上好吃，但分量很足，而且比外面便宜得多。打菜的阿姨似乎理解这里每个人的处境，手抖得格外慷慨，每次都多给半勺。墙上贴着「请勿浪费粮食」的标语，但没有人在意，因为在这里浪费食物通常不是因为挑剔，而是因为没有胃口。最安静的时刻是下午两三点，午饭的高峰过去了，食堂里只剩零星几个人。阳光从窗户斜斜地照进来，照在空荡荡的桌椅上。一个食堂，本来只是一个吃饭的地方，但在医院里，它承载着焦虑、疲惫和微弱的希望。人们在这里短暂地补充能量，然后回到各自的担忧中去。
text_pinyin: Yīyuàn de shítáng hé bié de shítáng bù yíyàng. Zhèlǐ de rén chīfàn shí jīhū bù shuōhuà, jíbiàn shuōhuà yě yādī le shēngyīn, hǎoxiàng shēngyīn dà yìdiǎn jiù huì jīngrǎo dào shénme. Chuān bái dàguà de yīshēng sānwǔ-chéngqún de zuò zài yìqǐ, yòng zuì kuài de sùdù bǎ fàn bā jìn zuǐ lǐ, ránhòu cā cā zuǐ jiù zǒu le. Tāmen de shǒushù fú shàng ǒu'ěr hái néng kàn dào méi lái de jí huàn xià de shǒutào hénjì. Bìngrén jiāshǔ tōngcháng zuò zài kào chuāng de wèizhì, miànqián de fàncài dòng le jǐ kǒu jiù bù chī le, yǎnjing yìzhí dīng zhe shǒujī, děng zhe mǒu gè diànhuà huòzhě xiāoxi. Yǒuxiē rén de yǎnquān shì hóng de, dàn méiyǒu rén kū chū shēng lái. Shítáng de càipǐn tán bú shàng hǎochī, dàn fènliàng hěn zú, érqiě bǐ wàimiàn piányi de duō. Dǎ cài de āyí sìhū lǐjiě zhèlǐ měi gè rén de chǔjìng, shǒu dǒu de géwài kāngkǎi, měi cì dōu duō gěi bàn sháo. Qiáng shàng tiē zhe 「qǐng wù làngfèi liángshi」 de biāoyǔ, dàn méiyǒu rén zàiyì, yīnwèi zài zhèlǐ làngfèi shíwù tōngcháng bú shì yīnwèi tiāoti, ér shì yīnwèi méiyǒu wèikǒu. Zuì ānjìng de shíkè shì xiàwǔ liǎng sān diǎn, wǔfàn de gāofēng guòqù le, shítáng lǐ zhǐ shèng língxīng jǐ gè rén. Yángguāng cóng chuānghù xiéxié de zhào jìnlái, zhào zài kōngdàngdàng de zhuōyǐ shàng. Yí gè shítáng, běnlái zhǐ shì yí gè chīfàn de dìfang, dàn zài yīyuàn lǐ, tā chéngzài zhe jiāolǜ, píbèi hé wēiruò de xīwàng. Rénmen zài zhèlǐ duǎnzàn de bǔchōng néngliàng, ránhòu huí dào gèzì de dānyōu zhōng qù.
text_en: A hospital cafeteria is different from other cafeterias. People here barely speak while eating, and when they do, they keep their voices low, as if any loudness might disturb something. Doctors in white coats sit in small groups, shoveling food down as fast as possible, then wiping their mouths and leaving. Occasionally you can still see glove marks on their surgical scrubs that they hadn't time to change out of. Patients' family members usually sit by the windows, picking at their food for a few bites before stopping, eyes fixed on their phones, waiting for some call or message. Some have red-rimmed eyes, but nobody cries aloud. The food isn't particularly good, but the portions are generous and much cheaper than outside. The serving auntie seems to understand everyone's situation here, her hand trembling with extra generosity, always giving an extra half-scoop. A sign on the wall reads 'Please don't waste food,' but nobody pays it mind, because here food goes to waste not from pickiness but from lack of appetite. The quietest moment is around two or three in the afternoon, after the lunch rush, when only a scattering of people remain. Sunlight slants through the windows onto the empty tables and chairs. A cafeteria is supposed to be just a place to eat, but in a hospital it carries anxiety, exhaustion, and fragile hope. People briefly refuel here, then return to their respective worries.
